questions.question: Explain the role of the reduction clause in parallelizing the trapezoidal rule with OpenMP. How does it ensure correctness?
keyPoints: The reduction(+: integral) clause creates a private copy of integral for each thread, initialized to 0
Each thread independently accumulates its share of f(x_i) values into its private copy
At the end of the parallel region, OpenMP automatically sums all private copies into the original variable
This avoids race conditions without requiring explicit locks or critical sections

questions.question: Write and explain the complete OpenMP implementation of the trapezoidal rule using the parallel for directive with reduction. Compare it with the critical section approach.
keyPoints: Sequential base: compute h = (b-a)/n, initialize integral = (f(a)+f(b))/2, loop from i=1 to n-1 adding f(a+i*h)
Parallel version: add #pragma omp parallel for reduction(+:integral) before the loop
Variable scoping: a, b, n, h are shared; i is automatically private; integral is reduction variable
Critical approach: uses #pragma omp critical inside the loop instead of reduction
Critical is much slower because it serializes the accumulation (one thread at a time per iteration)
Reduction is preferred: compiler-optimized, O(log p) combination overhead, no explicit synchronization

questions.question: Describe the variable scoping rules that apply to the OpenMP trapezoidal rule. Which variables are shared, private, and why?
keyPoints: a, b, n, h are shared: input parameters and derived constants, read-only by all threads
Loop index i is automatically private in parallel for
x_i declared inside the loop is private by default (stack-allocated per thread)
integral uses the reduction clause: private copies combined via addition at the end

questions.question: Compare the OpenMP and MPI approaches to parallelizing the trapezoidal rule. Discuss the memory model, communication, ease of programming, and scalability of each approach.
keyPoints: OpenMP uses shared memory; MPI uses distributed memory with message passing
OpenMP requires minimal code changes (add pragmas to sequential code); MPI requires rewriting with explicit send/recv/bcast/reduce
OpenMP uses reduction clause for result aggregation; MPI uses MPI_Reduce
OpenMP is limited to a single shared-memory node; MPI scales across multiple nodes
OpenMP has no communication overhead for parameter distribution; MPI requires MPI_Bcast
Both produce the same mathematical result; both achieve near-linear speedup for this embarrassingly parallel problem
